1: The letter opens with ho en ap arches — what was from the beginning — deliberately echoing John 1:1 (en arche en ho logos) while shifting from cosmological declaration to experiential testimony. The subject is not "in the beginning" as a time-point but "from the beginning" as a continuous reality now intersecting with apostolic history. The chain of verbs — heard, seen, looked at (etheasametha, the verb of prolonged beholding), handled (epselaphesen, physically touched) — builds from the most remote sense to the most intimate, asserting the full material reality of the incarnation against any spiritualizing tendency.
7: haima (haima) — blood functions here as covenant-cleansing agent in the Levitical sense: not merely moral example or martyrdom but the substance that purifies from sin. katharizei (katharizei) is present tense — ongoing, continuous cleansing, not once-accomplished and finished. apo pases hamartias — from every sin — the scope is universal and unqualified. The phrase haima Iesou tou huiou autou is theologically dense: Jesus (the human name) is the Son (divine relation), making the blood both fully human (real sacrifice) and of infinite worth (divine person).
9: pistos kai dikaios — faithful and just — pairs two divine attributes as the theological ground for forgiveness. Faithfulness (pistos) is covenant-fidelity to the promise of forgiveness; justice (dikaios) is the legal-moral attribute that might seem to demand punishment but here grounds forgiveness because the atonement has satisfied justice. God forgives justly — not by overlooking sin but because Christ has borne its penalty. afeisin (aphiemi) = to release, cancel; katharize = to purify, cleanse — two complementary images: legal cancellation and cultic purification.
2:
ἐφανερώθη (ephanerōthē) — aorist passive of φανερόω, "was manifested." The verb appears twice in this verse, bookending the parenthetical testimony. It is a key term in 1 John (used again at 3:2,5,8; 4:9) and signals that what was hidden has been disclosed — not that something new came into being, but that what already existed became publicly visible.

τὴν ζωὴν τὴν αἰώνιον — "the eternal life" (double article, emphatic). The definite article marks this not as a quality or concept but as a specific, identifiable reality that "was with the Father." The phrase πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ("with the Father") uses the same preposition as John 1:1 ("with God"), suggesting personal presence and relation rather than mere existence.
3:
κοινωνία (koinōnia, G2842) — rendered "fellowship" in the MKT, but the Greek root carries the sense of active sharing, partnership, and participation — more than social togetherness. The double occurrence ("fellowship with us... our fellowship is with the Father") maps the vertical and horizontal together: participation in the apostolic witness is simultaneously participation in the Father–Son relation.

The purpose clause ἵνα καὶ ὑμεῖς κοινωνίαν ἔχητε μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν — "so that you also may have fellowship with us" — places the Johannine community as the mediating link. The reader cannot bypass the apostolic witness to reach the divine fellowship directly.
4: χαρά (chara) — "joy." The pronoun is contested: some manuscripts read ἡμῶν ("our joy") and others ὑμῶν ("your joy"). The MKT follows "our joy" — the community whose proclamation and whose readers' reception together complete the joy of the eyewitnesses. πεπληρωμένη ᾖ — present perfect passive subjunctive: "may be (and remain) made full." Completeness of joy is the stated goal of writing, placing the letter's purpose within the tradition of Johannine joy language (cf. John 15:11; 16:24; 17:13).
5:
θεὸς φῶς ἐστιν — "God is light." The predicate noun φῶς (light) is anarthrous — it identifies the character of God without reducing him to an abstraction. In Greek predicate-noun constructions, the anarthrous noun specifies the category or quality; the subject retains its definite identity. The statement is absolute: not "God is like light" or "God gives light" but "God = light" in his essential character.

σκοτία ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν οὐδεμία — the double negative is emphatic: "darkness in him is not, not even any." The MKT renders "no darkness at all," correctly capturing the intensified negation. Darkness is not a partial condition or a limited defect — it is utterly absent.
6:
This verse opens the first of six ἐὰν εἴπωμεν ("if we say") conditions in chapters 1–2 (1:6,8,10; 2:4,6,9). Each pairs a false verbal claim with a behavioral reality that contradicts it. The present tense περιπατῶμεν ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ — "we walk in darkness" — signals habitual, ongoing conduct, not isolated acts.

ψευδόμεθα — present "we lie," not "we were mistaken." The verdict is active moral falsehood. οὐ ποιοῦμεν τὴν ἀλήθειαν — "we do not do (practice) the truth." In Johannine usage, truth is not merely believed but enacted — a behavioral category as much as an epistemic one.
8: ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ ἔχομεν — "we have no sin." The claim is about possessing or carrying sin as a present condition, not about committing specific sinful acts (contrast v.10, "we have not sinned"). The present-tense πλανῶμεν — "we deceive" — is reflexive: the deception is self-directed. ἡ ἀλήθεια οὐκ ἔστιν ἐν ἡμῖν — "the truth is not in us." Truth (ἡ ἀλήθεια, with article) is not a proposition but a living reality with personal character in Johannine usage.
10: ἡμαρτήκαμεν — perfect "we have sinned" — not the present sinful condition of v.8 but the denial of specific, completed sinful acts: claiming a clean record, not merely a clean present state. ψεύστην ποιοῦμεν αὐτόν — "we make him a liar" — the charge against God himself, not merely an error. The claim that one has never sinned directly contradicts the divine testimony. ὁ λόγος αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐν ἡμῖν — "his word is not in us" — an absence of indwelling personal reality, not merely intellectual disagreement.
